title: Bacon sandwich
subtitle: Crisp back bacon in white bread with butter and ketchup
excerpt: The bacon sandwich is the British weekend morning at its simplest. Back bacon grilled until the fat crisps, put in buttered white bread with ketchup or brown sauce. There is very little to explain and very little to go wrong.
sourceNotes: British breakfast staple. Back bacon in white bread is one of the most consumed breakfasts in the country, found in cafes, at home, and in every institution canteen. The back bacon sandwich has been a working-class British staple since at least the early 20th century.

content.text: A bacon sandwich requires back bacon, not streaky. Back bacon gives the lean and the fat together, and the fat should crisp at the edges while the lean stays tender. Soft white bread and butter are non-negotiable. The choice of ketchup or brown sauce is entirely your own.

items.prepNote: thick-cut soft white, or 2 soft white rolls

content.text: Heat a frying pan over a medium-high heat. Add the bacon rashers in a single layer. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes per side until the fat is crisp and the lean is cooked through. Remove and rest for a moment.
Butter the bread. Lay three rashers on two slices, add your condiment, and top with the remaining bread. Press gently and eat immediately while the bacon is still hot.

items.symptom: The bacon is tough and chewy
items.cause: Overcooked, or streaky bacon used instead of back.
items.fix: Use back bacon and do not cook past the point where the fat is just crisp. A minute less is better than a minute more.

items.symptom: The fat is soft and floppy
items.fix: Cook over a medium-high heat so the fat renders and crisps. Soft fat is the most common complaint about a bacon sandwich.

items.cause: Bacon placed on the bread while still dripping fat.
items.fix: Rest the bacon on the pan for 30 seconds after cooking, then transfer to the bread.

content.text: The British bacon sandwich is as much a cultural fixture as a recipe. It appears at every Saturday morning, every motorway service station, and every building site breakfast van. There are strong views about the choice of condiment, the type of bread, and whether the bacon should be grilled or fried. The basics, though, never change: back bacon, white bread, butter, and something from a bottle.
